Reading the futures curve: a market-timing layer for crop decisions

Forward-curve analytics as decision support for crop marketing, storage, procurement and hedge-timing — designed to sit alongside field intelligence, not replace it.

17th International Conference on Precision Agriculture (ICPA) & 11th Brazilian Congress on Precision and Digital Agriculture (ConBAP) · Porto Alegre, Brazil · July 13–16, 2026

Remote sensing, yield models and soil data answer “what is happening in the field?” The futures curve answers a different question: “how is the market pricing timing, storage and supply risk?” We use the curve’s shape to identify the days when that market signal is unusually informative for a store-vs-sell decision.

Soybean accuracy, high-confidence days
66%
+11 pt vs the 55% base rate, out-of-sample
Alerts per year
~5
deliberately rare — high-conviction only
Crops screened honestly
5
3 work, 2 reported as not working

The idea in one picture

A steep upward-sloping curve means the market is paying you to store grain and sell later; a flat or inverted curve means the market is telling you to sell now. Our model — a curve-shape factor model re-estimated as history accumulates, benchmarked against a random walk — predicts whether that storage reward will grow or shrink over the next month.

Act only on high-confidence days

The model does not treat every day equally. It ranks days by the size of the predicted move and only flags high-confidence days, when the curve is far from its historical shape and a larger adjustment is expected. These alerts are deliberately rare — for soybeans, roughly a handful of days per year (74 days across ~14 years of out-of-sample history): infrequent, but worth listening to.

Out-of-sample results across five crops

All results are from rolling out-of-sample validation: the model never sees the future when it predicts, and accuracy is always quoted against the all-days base rate — the edge is the gap, not the headline number. Crops where the method does not work are reported as such.

All experiments to date run on US and Canadian exchange data — CBOT futures (Chicago) for wheat, corn and soybeans, ICE Canada futures for canola. No Brazilian price series enters the models yet, by design: establishing how these signals transmit to Brazilian markets is the pilot’s job. Out-of-sample periods differ by crop because data history differs.

CropHigh-confidence accuracyAll-days baseSampleOut-of-sample periodStatus
Soybean66.2% (p=0.0035)55.2%n=742011–2024 (≈14 y)Lead result — Brazil’s #1 row crop
SRW wheat~73%57.8%n≈2312011–2024 (≈14 y)Benchmark — deep, liquid market
Canola~71%59.0%n≈832020–2024 (≈5 y)Supporting — strong seasonal structure
Corn~54% (p=0.26)51.3%n=852011–2024 (≈14 y)Research track — safrinha seasonality next
HRW wheat~coin flip48.8%—2015–2024 (≈10 y)Negative control — rejected by the screen

Corn’s weak result is plausibly partly a calendar mismatch: Brazil’s safrinha cycle differs from the US cycle embedded in CBOT corn. Adding a seasonal target already lifts corn’s unconditional accuracy by +7.3 pt — Brazil-specific seasonality is the named research direction.

Who is this for

The same validated alert stream, four ways. Click a card for a worked case study from the out-of-sample backtests.

Cooperatives & producers An alert that market timing risk is elevated — a prompt to review storage, sale timing or an open hedge, in plain language. Case study: soybean store-vs-sell timing →
Grain buyers & processors A monitor for when deferred-vs-nearby pricing pressure is shifting — a prompt to pull purchases forward or stay hand-to-mouth. Case study: wheat procurement timing →
Agri-tech platforms & researchers A market-risk layer for an existing advisory product, and a declared, causally validated market-structure variable to test alongside agronomic data. Case study: the alert stream itself →
Futures & spread traders The most direct use: trade the predicted change in the calendar spread — with costs and negative controls reported honestly. Case study: canola spread P&L net of costs →

Pairs with field intelligence — and we already run both layers

A companion satellite pipeline (MODIS + Google Earth Engine) estimates state/district-level yields across five crop-regions, including Brazil Mato Grosso soybean and corn. The monitoring layer works, and it is early where it matters: a safrinha-corn forecast lands months before any municipal official number in Brazil, and in the US and Canada the same pipeline runs ~4 weeks ahead of USDA corn and 36 days ahead of Statistics Canada on canola. Where official reporting is already fast — US wheat, and Brazilian soybean, which CONAB and IBGE cover monthly — we report no timing edge. The edge lives where official reporting is slow.

Crop & regionYield accuracy (OOS)First useful forecastFirst official estimateTiming edge
Soybean — Mato Grosso, Brazil~5% (trend-led)mid-Nov (pre-harvest)CONAB / IBGE monthly in-seasonminimal — official already monthly; satellite not required here (our call)
Corn — Mato Grosso, Brazil~9% MAEmid-May (pre-harvest)final statistics months latermonths (muni-level official-silent); CONAB comparison pending
Corn — Iowa, US~5% MAEmid-JulyUSDA, ~Aug 12~4 weeks before USDA
Canola — Canada (SK/MB)6–10% MAEJul 15Statistics Canada, ~Aug 2036 days before StatCan
Winter wheat — US5–7% MAEMayUSDA, May (in-season)none — USDA is fast and accurate

Accuracy is out-of-sample MAE as % of median yield at state/district level (expanding-window validation, no look-ahead). Canola figures are for Saskatchewan + Manitoba, ~90% of Canadian production.

Part of what we bring is knowing, per crop, whether the satellite layer earns its keep or the yield trend alone suffices — a proprietary judgment, not a one-size-fits-all claim. For volatile, weather-driven crops (corn, canola) the satellite adds materially. For a stable, high-technology crop like Mato Grosso soybean, the yield is largely trend-predictable, so we deliver an accurate forecast without depending on the satellite. That distinction — where the field layer helps and where it does not — is exactly the kind of call that separates a real decision system from a dashboard.

The honest second finding: on the current small sample, the satellite yield signal has not yet added measurable price-prediction power beyond the curve signal alone. Quantifying that combination on Brazilian data is exactly the open research question this community is best placed to attack.

What we claim

  • The forward curve carries useful information about short-term crop-market timing risk.
  • The signal is strongest when conditioned on model confidence.
  • Soybean shows a robust, statistically significant real-time result.
  • Negative controls are built in — the framework rejects unsuitable markets.

What we don’t claim

  • That the method works on all crops — corn is ongoing research.
  • That it replaces agronomic knowledge or local judgment.
  • That CBOT/ICE results transfer one-for-one to Brazilian local prices — transmission to local basis, B3 contracts and BRL/USD is untested, and is precisely what a Brazil pilot would establish.

A Brazil pilot

  • Lead with soybean; SRW wheat as the large-sample benchmark; corn as a research track.
  • Test price transmission: CBOT signal vs B3 futures and local cash/basis series (e.g. Mato Grosso, Paraná), including BRL/USD effects.
  • Add Brazil-specific layers: regional yield outlook, weather stress, logistics, export basis, safrinha calendars — extending the existing satellite pipeline (already covering Mato Grosso corn) to soybeans.
  • Prototype a cooperative-facing dashboard: current signal, confidence level, historical analogues, crop-specific reliability label, plain-language context.

Let’s talk in Porto Alegre

We are here to find partners, not to sell a finished product. We would like to meet:

  • Pilot partners — cooperatives and agri-tech platforms with Brazilian growers who would test market-risk alerts in a real advisory workflow.
  • Data partners — access to B3 futures and local cash/basis series (Mato Grosso, Paraná) to test how the signal transmits to farm-gate prices.
  • Research collaborators — combining the curve signal with yield, weather and remote-sensing layers on Brazilian data.

Kenny Cheung · kenny@algo-rythmn.ai · algo-rythmn.ai

All results are out-of-sample on US (CBOT) and Canadian (ICE) futures data; no Brazilian price series is used at this stage. Accuracy figures are directional (1-month spread forecasts) and always paired with their base rate; sample sizes and p-values as shown. Prepared by Algo-Rythmn (algo-rythmn.ai) for ICPA & ConBAP 2026, Porto Alegre · Contact: Kenny Cheung <kenny@algo-rythmn.ai>

Case study · CBOT soybean · 2011–2024 out-of-sample

Cooperatives & producers — timing the store-vs-sell decision

The baseline is what most co-ops already do: market grain on a fixed schedule through the season. The case study changes nothing except on alert days, and each decision is scored on how the storage reward actually moved over the following month, versus sticking to the schedule.

Model signalWhat you doWhy
Carry building
(storage reward to grow)		Store — and sell deferred futures against the grain (a carrying-charge hedge)The market is paying you to store; capture it
Tightening
(storage reward to shrink)		Move grain sooner — sell nearby, unwind storage hedgesDon’t pay to store into a collapsing carry
No alert (most days)Follow the normal marketing planThe model only speaks when it is confident
Decisions flagged
36
~2.6 per year over ≈14 years
Direction correct
64%
vs the 55% all-days base rate
Avg captured per decision
+28 ¢/bu
≈ US$10 per tonne held or released

Read this before quoting the numbers

  • 83% of the alerts were “store” calls, and part of that gain is the market’s persistent carry structure — not pure timing skill. We say so rather than hide it.
  • 2011–2013 was net negative — the edge is real but not uniform; it pays for acting consistently across many alerts, not for any single call.
  • Measured on the exchange curve. Local basis, physical storage cost, quality and cash-flow constraints are on top of this and are not modelled.
  • Decision support, not hedging advice — the marketing decision stays with the co-op.

Case study · CBOT SRW wheat · 2011–2024 out-of-sample

Grain buyers & processors — timing forward coverage

The baseline is a buyer covering needs on a fixed monthly schedule. On alert days the schedule flexes, and each re-timed decision is scored on how the near-vs-deferred cost actually moved over the following month.

Model signalWhat you doWhy
Tightening
(nearby premium to build)		Lock in now — pull coverage forwardAvoid paying a bigger nearby premium later; buy before scarcity is priced in
Carry building
(deferred premium to build)		Wait — stay hand-to-mouthNo reward for committing forward yet; the carry does the waiting for you
No alert (most days)Stick to the normal scheduleThe model only speaks when it is confident

Illustration: a processor buying 100,000 t/yr that re-times about one month of coverage per alert would have kept roughly US$146k per year at the averages below — an illustration of scale, not a guarantee.

Decisions flagged
42
~3.1 per year over ≈14 years
Direction correct
86%
89% on “wait” calls · 79% on “lock in now”
Avg cost avoided
+15.5 ¢/bu
≈ US$5.7 per tonne re-timed

Read this before quoting the numbers

  • This measures the timing component (the near-vs-deferred relationship). Flat-price moves hit the fixed schedule and the flexed schedule alike, so they are deliberately not part of the claim.
  • Both directions worked on wheat — this is not just riding the carry.
  • Exchange futures only: local basis, freight and storage logistics are not modelled. The annual-savings figure is an illustration with stated assumptions.
  • SRW is the deep, liquid benchmark market; results differ by crop — see the five-crop table above.

Case study · the alert stream as a product / research feed

Agri-tech platforms & researchers — what you would actually integrate

The deliverable is a daily feed: current curve shape, a plain-language direction call (“storage reward likely to grow / shrink over the next month”), a confidence score, and a rare high-conviction alert flag. It drops into an existing advisory product alongside yield, weather and remote-sensing modules — and for researchers it is a declared, causally validated market-structure variable to test against agronomic data.

Model signalWhat you doWhy
Carry buildingPush a plain-language prompt: “the market is paying to store — review storage / hedge options”A timely advisory touchpoint, no finance jargon
TighteningPrompt: “the market is bidding for grain now — review sale timing”Same alert, seller-side framing
No alert (most days)Show status quo — no notificationRare, high-conviction alerts protect user trust; no alarm fatigue
Days flagged
14%
of all trading days — flagged causally, no hindsight
Distinct episodes
~2.6 / yr
alerts cluster around dislocations
Direction correct
64%
on soybean episodes — wrong ~1 in 3, and users must know that

Read this before quoting the numbers

  • Alerts cluster: a dislocation persists for days, so raw daily flags overstate how many independent decisions there are. The figure shows distinct episodes.
  • An advisory product must carry the error rate honestly — ~1 call in 3 is wrong, and the value comes from consistency across alerts.
  • The same feed is validated per crop; crops that fail the screen (HRW wheat) are reported as failing — a platform can trust the label.
  • Integration is an API/flat-file feed; no farm data leaves the platform.

Case study · ICE canola calendar spread · 2020–2024 out-of-sample

Futures & spread traders — the signal as a position, net of costs

The most direct use: trade the 9m−1m calendar spread in the predicted direction. Enter the next day (no look-ahead), hold 20 trading days. Costs are charged per spread crossing as a multiple of the spread’s daily volatility σ.

Model signalWhat you doWhy
Carry buildingLong the calendar spread — buy far, sell nearProfit if the spread widens as predicted
TighteningShort the calendar spread — sell far, buy nearProfit if the spread narrows as predicted
No alert (most days)No positionThe edge exists only on high-confidence days

How the edge holds up as assumed costs rise:

Assumed costNet per tradeSharpe
0.25σ per crossing+20.3 CAD/t0.98
0.50σ per crossing+15.3 CAD/t0.84
1.00σ per crossing+5.3 CAD/t0.55
See the full trading-simulation results across all five crops, net of costs →
Trades
371
daily entries, 20-day overlapping holds
Win rate
74%
profit factor 7.1 — wins run larger than losses
Survives 1σ costs
Yes
the only crop of the five that clearly does

Read this before quoting the numbers

  • Canola is the only crop of five where the P&L clearly survives conservative costs — SRW wheat turns negative at 1σ. We publish that, not just the winner.
  • P&L is on the constant-maturity interpolated spread, not executed contract legs; deferred ag legs are less liquid than this assumes.
  • Positions overlap (up to 20 concurrent), which inflates Sharpe and t-stats; treat risk-adjusted figures as upper bounds.
  • Part of the cross-crop gross P&L is structural carry rather than directional skill — our negative-control crops also profit gross of costs, and we flag that as a red flag, not a feature.

Trading simulation · all five crops · out-of-sample, CBOT / ICE data

Detailed trading-simulation results

Each crop’s high-confidence signal is turned into a calendar-spread position — enter the next day (no look-ahead), hold 20 trading days — and the P&L is restated in currency per contract-spread (CBOT bushel crops: 1 ¢/bu = US$50 per contract; canola: 1 CAD/t = CAD 20 per contract). Costs are charged per spread crossing as a multiple of the spread’s daily volatility σ; the headline figures use 0.25σ/crossing.

CropTradesWin%PFSharpe Total netMax drawdownnet/trade
@ 0.00σ		net/trade
@ 0.25σ		net/trade
@ 0.50σ		net/trade
@ 1.00σ
Soybean (CBOT)49861%2.690.33+599,787 USD-328,402 USD+1,320+995+671+22
SRW wheat (CBOT)36978%4.750.61+210,645 USD-29,803 USD+724+495+266-193
Canola (ICE)37174%7.080.98+163,403 CAD-25,574 CAD+506+406+306+106
Corn (CBOT) — negative control46158%2.630.37+470,366 USD-136,196 USD+1,078+891+703+328
HRW wheat (CBOT) — negative control39660%2.250.23+140,808 USD-164,759 USD+437+233+30-378

The net/trade columns show per-trade P&L at each assumed cost level — a negative value means the edge does not survive that cost.

How to read these honestly

  • Two of the five crops — corn and HRW wheat — are built-in negative controls. They also show a gross profit, which we read as a warning that part of the P&L is structural carry rather than directional skill — not as a second success. We show them rather than hide them.
  • Only canola clearly survives the conservative 1σ-per-crossing cost; SRW wheat turns negative there. The net/trade columns show exactly where each crop’s edge breaks.
  • Positions overlap (up to 20 concurrent), which inflates Sharpe and significance; treat the risk-adjusted figures as upper bounds.
  • P&L is on the constant-maturity interpolated spread, not executed contract legs — the deferred agricultural legs are less liquid than this assumes.
